finding: A demographic attribute influenced how people label individuals; those labeled by name alone were perceived as more eminent and deserving of status-linked benefits.
People underestimate the rate at which judgments form, leading them to acquire and share more information than decision-making actually requires.
Psychophysiological reactions showed a systematic bias toward negative information across multiple countries, with considerable individual variation.
Diversity in relationship types predicted subjective well-being above and beyond total social interaction frequency.
A cognitive prime reduced reliance on human judgment and increased acceptance of algorithmic recommendations, mediated by perceptions of human fallibility.
No direct priming effect from evaluation to production was found, but production-to-production priming was observed; verb-construction compatibility influenced acceptability ratings.
A linguistic feature reduced competence and comprehensibility ratings, with effects moderated by job prestige and performance context.
Proficiency level and error subtypes revealed previously undetected patterns in morpheme emergence order, partially confirming but refining prior findings.
Perceptions of economic security predicted sense of meaning independent of income; security threats undermined meaning judgments.
Emotional cues triggered better recall of neutral background details than neutral cues did, contrary to narrowed-encoding predictions; memory traces were effectively formed.
A visual feature's effect on emotion and health perception was context-dependent, moderated by facial expression and structure morphology.
One emotion category produced enhanced memory over another; attention mediated this difference when stimulus organization was controlled.
A personality trait predicted attentional bias toward threat cues, with timing parameters showing no evidence of initial vigilance followed by avoidance.
Evaluative considerations about behavior (moral qualities) influenced whether people classified actions as intentional.
Rejection of objectivity claim did not eliminate typical patterns in moral judgment; core distinctions were preserved.
Moral features of actions influenced intentionality judgments, outweighing or overriding considerations of skill and control.
Lay people judged agents in deterministic scenarios as acting freely and morally responsible, contrary to some philosophical predictions.
Perceived moral attributes influenced third-person ascriptions of internal states; intrinsic biases in mindreading explained attribution patterns.
Both atypicality and moral valence influenced causal judgments; atypicality could not be dismissed as an alternative explanation.
Reluctance in a choice scenario was better explained by status quo bias than by a preference for reality contact over virtual experience.
